I do check the “wiki” website, but after pulling back from buying WB’s to age due to premox, I don’t have much to post…but I do wish I had posted more in years past when I was dumping so many bottles down the sink.

My suggestion, though, was directed more towards William, as well as other wine writers, who I assume taste more aged white Burgs than all but a small number of us…between bottles opened while visiting domaines, wine tasting dinners and events, restaurants, etc. A more vast experience than many of us can bring to bear on the problem.

My point was that this would harness an extremely valuable and more extensive collection of anecdotal data, and would not need to be presented in such a way that producers would take offense. It would be best for this to be an ongoing frequently updated feature on the writer’s website, in the same way that the wiki site is/was frequently added to, in the same way that Burghound’s database of personal tasting notes is (not often enough, unfortunately) updated, etc.
This would be more useful than waiting for a retrospective article to be published once a year.

And perhaps such an ongoing 'premox or WB watch" would have the beneficial effect of providing feedback to the producers in a more publicly visible way, maybe influence those in denial, and inform those taking steps whether those are proving to be effective.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
David explained his theory to me and a well known winemaker from Meursault back in the mid 80s. When he was done, the French guy said, Hmm…I always wondered why the juice browned and then got clear again.

I don’t think Burgundians started to make Chardonnay like Freemark Abbey did in the 70s in 1996. I think the problems are
1/SO2
2/lack of glutathione in the lees and/or lack of lees
3/riper must, calling for more SO2…not less
4/less reductive wine

I don’t buy blaming the cork, but better seals could help.

posts.name: Don Cornwell
posts.cooked:
I don’t totally understand the science of all this, but I think David Ramey discusses something like this starting around minutes 7 or so of this interview. SoundCloud - Hear the world’s sounds

The presentation by David Ramey is excellent – particularly the portion from approx 2:30 to about 9 minutes in, where David explains what is technically called hyperoxidation or by the French “browning of the must” and really explains how it works. This podcast is from “I’ll drink to that” where the host interviews a number of winemakers. However, no it doesn’t answer the question of whether use of this technique somehow permanently changes the redox potential of the wine.

To my surprise, I also saw that the “I’ll drink to that” podcasts included an interview with Pierre Morey. SoundCloud - Hear the world’s sounds I decided I had to listen to it. No, there was no useful information on the sulfiting practices at Domaine Pierre Morey. Mostly it is a lot of history about Domaine Pierre Morey and their previous metayage arrangements with Domaine Lafon, and a lot of history about Pierre Morey’s work as regisseur of Domaine Leflaive and Leflaive becoming bio-dynamic. The only surprise was to learn that Domaine Leflaive moved the wines from barrel to tank at the end of 12 months simply because they didn’t have the room to continue to storage the wines in barrel, so that if the wines weren’t going to be bottled before the next vintage, there was no choice but to move them to stainless tanks.

Finally, beginning at 50 minutes into the interview, Morey discusses the oxidation of the 2006 Leflaive wines, his last vintage made there. He acknowledges that there was a big problem with oxidation of the 2006 wines but claims to have no idea as to what went wrong – and then goes on to speculate that maybe somehow excess humidity at the time of the vintage caused the problem, or some type of external environmental pollution caused the problem. He even suggests that maybe Roundup caused the problem because by then there was some detectable Roundup in the Puligny water table. I KID YOU NOT!!! Listen for yourself and see. (If that was the case, why weren’t all wines from all producers from the 2006 vintage on subject to premox problems? And how did the premox problems start 11 vintages earlier in 1995? ) There is no discussion of the excessive reduction problems that Leflaive had in 2004, no discussion of any changes in the sulfite usage or winemaking after 2004, and no discussion of horrendous premox problems that that Leflaive continued to have from 2007 through at least 2010. I’m sorry, but it is very hard for me to have any respect for the man after hearing these garbage excuses. His excuses are even worse than when Anne Claude was serving clearly oxidized bottles of Leflaive whites at the Paulee and claiming that they weren’t oxidized when people commented that the wine was oxidized.

This seems a bit of an extreme response, Emmanuel. That’s my opinion.

In view of what is now a 25-year old problem, consumers want candor, self-reflection, and a sense of responsibility—all of which many of us see as necessary to solve a problem that has caused probably 100s of millions of dollars of wasted consumer spending. If Don perceives a winemaker’s response as having a head-in-the-sand or denial viewpoint, or as providing explanations that seem off-base in view of the facts on the ground, it’s not unreasonable for Don to counter from his perspective publicly, to call it like he sees it, and to “talk” with his dollars. I would say most white Burg buyers on this board have valued Don’s service to public awareness of this issue.

I haven’t had enough Pierre Morey house wines to have a view personally on him. I do know his Leflaive wines through the early 2000s compared favorably to other houses based on my own and crowd sourced info. And he has been applauded for that work, not only here but from what I’ve seen throughout the white Burg-loving world. (Let’s put aside the irony that we now have to applaud winemakers who made white Burg that did not have premox problems.)

Free public discourse is the lifeblood of wine boards and democracy. You are also free to respond as you have.
